rubric_name: Android XR Low-Vision & High-Contrast Accessibility Specialization
purpose: Additional rules focused specifically on users with low vision, color vision deficiency, or light sensitivity, who need larger, higher-contrast, and less detail-dependent UI to use an Android XR app comfortably.
source: Android XR app quality guidelines, Android XR accessibility overview, Android XR visual design guidelines, and WCAG 2.1 contrast guidance as referenced by Android accessibility guidance.

rules.rule: Text and meaningful UI graphics must meet at least a 4.5:1 contrast ratio against their background (3:1 for large-scale text of 18pt+ regular or 14pt+ bold), per WCAG and Android accessibility guidance.
rules.why_it_matters: Below this ratio, low-vision users and users with light sensitivity may not be able to perceive text or icons at all, regardless of size.
rules.telemetry_signal: Not directly measurable from interaction telemetry; requires a static color-contrast check on each UI component's foreground/background pair.

rules.rule: While the platform baseline is 48dp minimum / 56dp optimal, apps should consider an enlarged or user-adjustable target size option for users who need it, rather than treating 48dp as sufficient for everyone.
rules.why_it_matters: Low-vision users may need meaningfully larger targets than the platform baseline to locate and hit controls reliably.
rules.telemetry_signal: Elevated failed grab_attempt rate or elevated distance_to_target specifically in sessions flagged with an accessibility_mode or low_vision_mode metadata field, compared to sessions without it.

rules.rule: Never use color as the sole way to convey status, selection, or meaning (for example red/green only); pair color with shape, icon, text label, or pattern differences.
rules.why_it_matters: Users with color vision deficiency cannot reliably distinguish meaning conveyed by color alone.
rules.telemetry_signal: Not directly measurable from interaction telemetry; requires a static review of any state or status indicator that currently relies on color as its only differentiator.

rules.rule: App layouts should respect and scale correctly with system-level text size and display size settings rather than using fixed, unscalable text and UI elements.
rules.why_it_matters: Android XR accessibility settings let users increase text size and display size; an app that ignores these settings leaves low-vision users without their expected accommodation.
rules.telemetry_signal: ui_interaction events showing text or layout elements that overlap, clip, or become unreadable when the session metadata indicates a non-default text_scale or display_scale setting.

rules.rule: The app's UI should remain fully legible and functional when the system's High Contrast Fonts or Relumino outline accessibility features are enabled.
rules.why_it_matters: These are real user-facing Android XR accessibility settings; if custom app styling overrides or conflicts with them, low-vision users lose the accommodation they turned on.
rules.telemetry_signal: Increased hesitation, failed grab_attempts, or long time-to-first-interaction specifically in sessions where session metadata indicates high_contrast_mode or relumino_outline is enabled, compared to sessions without.

rules.rule: Do not require users to perceive fine visual detail (thin lines, small distant text, subtle texture differences) to understand or operate a control.
rules.why_it_matters: Users with low vision may perceive blur or missing detail even when an element is technically large enough; controls that depend on fine detail to be understood will fail for them regardless of size.
rules.telemetry_signal: Repeated failed grab_attempt or long hesitation on components with known fine-detail styling. Cross-reference against icon_button_visual_clarity from the base rubric.

rules.rule: Avoid sudden, jarring brightness changes, flashing elements, or overly bright environments; support the system's Color Inversion setting without visual conflicts.
rules.why_it_matters: Users with light sensitivity or migraine-related vision effects can experience discomfort or be unable to continue using the app if this is not respected.
rules.telemetry_signal: Not directly measurable from interaction telemetry; requires a static review of any flashing, strobing, or high-brightness transition in the app.

rules.rule: Where possible, support personalization aids such as zoom, adjustable contrast, or visual identifiers and outlines around important objects, so low-vision users can locate and perceive controls themselves.
rules.why_it_matters: Personalization lets users with a range of visual capability self-adjust the experience rather than relying on a single fixed design to work for everyone.
rules.telemetry_signal: Cross-reference target_discoverability findings from the base rubric against sessions with low_vision_mode metadata; if discoverability issues concentrate in those sessions, personalization aids are likely missing or ineffective.

rules.rule: Critical actions and feedback should have a non-visual fallback (audio cue, TTS description, or TalkBack-compatible label) rather than depending on vision alone.
rules.why_it_matters: For users with significant vision loss, a purely visual confirmation (for example a subtle color change) is not perceivable at all; an audio or spoken alternative ensures the action is still confirmed.
rules.telemetry_signal: Cross-reference press_feedback_confirmation and dead_control_no_response findings from the base rubric against sessions with screen_reader_enabled or low_vision_mode metadata.

schema_additions_for_accessibility_speci.note: Most rules above require session-level metadata that the base telemetry schema does not currently include. Add an optional accessibility_settings object to each session with keys such as low_vision_mode, text_scale, display_scale, high_contrast_mode, relumino_outline, and screen_reader_enabled.

output_format_for_agent.evidence_from_telemetry: specific numbers/events, or note static review required if detection_method is static_design_review
